Software 'SameCoin': SameCoin can list all system files and can avoid wiping specific directories such as Program Files, Windows, and Users. Software 'ChChes': ChChes collects the victim's %TEMP% directory path and version of Internet Explorer. Software 'Pasam': Pasam creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can retrieve lists of files. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can recursively enumerate files in an operator-provided directory. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use a file exfiltration tool to collect recently changed files with specific extensions. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can list information about files in a directory and recently opened or used documents. InvisiMole can also search for specific files by supplied file mask. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon lists the directories for Desktop, program files, and the user’s recently accessed files. Software 'Playcrypt': Playcrypt can avoid encrypting files with a .PLAY, .exe, .msi, .dll, .lnk, or .sys file extension. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to search for .txt, .ppt, .hwp, .pdf, and .doc files in specified directories. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb's loader can check for the FoggyWeb backdoor .pri file on a compromised AD FS server. Software 'Cannon': Cannon can obtain victim drive information as well as a list of folders in C:\Program Files. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu searches for various filesystem paths to determine what banking applications are installed on the victim’s machine. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker can enumerate files and directories just prior to encryption. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor can obtain a list of all files and directories as well as logical drives. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list file and directory information. Software 'Akira _v2': Akira _v2 can target specific files and folders for encryption. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea collects information about available drives, default browser, desktop file list, My Documents, Internet history, program files, and root of available drives. It also searches for ICS-related software files. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi is capable of obtaining directory, file, and drive listings. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape searches for the Kubernetes config file and other related files using a regular expression. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'MobileOrder': MobileOrder has a command to upload to its C2 server information about files on the victim mobile device, including SD card size, installed app list, SMS content, contacts, and calling history. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can enumerate files and collect associated metadata. Software 'ccf32': ccf32 can parse collected files to identify specific file extensions. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to index files from drives, user profiles, and removable drives. Software 'Prestige': Prestige can traverse the file system to discover files to encrypt by identifying specific extensions defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects information about directory and file structures, including total number of subdirectories, total number of files, and total size of files on infected systems. Software 'SOUNDBITE': SOUNDBITE is capable of enumerating and manipulating files and directories. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can check for the existence of files, including its own components, as well as retrieve a list of logical drives. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can exclude specific directories and files from encryption. Software 'StreamEx': StreamEx has the ability to enumerate drive types. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can search for specific files. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN can search, read, write, move, and execute files. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO has the ability to designate specific files and folders to encryption. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot searches the system for all of the following file extensions: .avi, .mov, .mkv, .mpeg, .mpeg4, .mp4, .mp3, .wav, .ogg, .jpeg, .jpg, .png, .bmp, .gif, .tiff, .ico, .xlsx, and .zip. It can also obtain browsing history, cookies, and plug-in information. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can search for files and directories. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has searched for .ldb and .log files stored in browser extension directories for collection and exfiltration. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can retrieve file listings from the victim machine. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to gather metadata from a file and to search for file and directory names. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has used Windows API to identify files associated with Windows Defender and Kaspersky. Software 'Kasidet': Kasidet has the ability to search for a given filename on a victim. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can collect directory and file lists. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple Directory Lister module can list information about files and directories including creation time, last write time, name, and size. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT has the ability to collect drive and file information on an infected machine. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun can enumerate information about files from the infected system, including file size, attributes, creation time, last access time, and write time. Rising Sun can enumerate the compilation timestamp of Windows executable files. Software 'SPACESHIP': SPACESHIP identifies files and directories for collection by searching for specific file extensions or file modification time. Software 'Penquin': Penquin can use the command code do_vslist to send file names, size, and status to C2. Software 'Machete': Machete produces file listings in order to search for files to be exfiltrated. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP can enumerate files on infected devices and avoid encrypting files with .exe, .dll, .sys, .lnk, or . lck extensions. Software 'RTM': RTM can check for specific files and directories associated with virtualization and malware analysis. Software 'KONNI': A version of KONNI searches for filenames created with a previous version of the malware, suggesting different versions targeted the same victims and the versions may work together. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can enumerate and search for files and directories. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can enumerate directories on a compromise host. Software 'AcidPour': AcidPour can identify specific files and directories within the Linux operating system corresponding with storage devices for follow-on wiping activity, similar to AcidRain. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has identified specific files and directories within targeted hosts and systems for modification, execution, collection and exfiltration. Software 'AcidRain': AcidRain identifies specific files and directories in the Linux operating system associated with storage devices. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can search a compromised host for specific files. Software 'Nebulae': Nebulae can list files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'FruitFly': FruitFly looks for specific files and file types. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET has used `mdfind` to enumerate a list of apps known to grant screen sharing permissions and leverages a module to run the command `ls -la ~/Desktop`. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can recursively search for files in folders and collects files from the desktop with certain extensions. Software 'PACEMAKER': PACEMAKER can parse `/proc/"process_name"/cmdline` to look for the string `dswsd` within the command line. Software 'Remexi': Remexi searches for files on the system. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can execute enum to enumerate files in storage on a compromised system. Software 'MultiLayer Wiper': MultiLayer Wiper generates a list of all files and paths on the fixed drives of an infected system, enumerating all files on the system except specific folders defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Cheerscrypt': Cheerscrypt can search for log and VMware-related files with .log, .vmdk, .vmem, .vswp, and .vmsn extensions. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information about the paths, size, and creation time of files with specific file extensions, but not the actual content of the file. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can target directories on victim machines for file collection. Software 'Dacls': Dacls can scan directories on a compromised host. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda searches for specific directories on the victim’s machine. Software 'Rover': Rover automatically searches for files on local drives based on a predefined list of file extensions. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can enumerate directories on target machines. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat can list directories. Software 'Pisloader': Pisloader has commands to list drives on the victim machine and to list file information for a given directory. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer identifies target files and directories for collection based on a configuration file. Software 'LITTLELAMB.WOOLTEA': LITTLELAMB.WOOLTEA can monitor for system upgrade events by checking for the presence of `/tmp/data/root/dev`. Software 'MacMa': MacMa can search for a specific file on the compromised computer and can enumerate files in Desktop, Downloads, and Documents folders. Software 'Royal': Royal can identify specific files and directories to exclude from the encryption process. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has searched the Program Files directories for specific folders and has searched for strings related to its mutexes. Software 'Megazord': Megazord can ignore specified directories for encryption. Software 'SharpDisco': SharpDisco can identify recently opened files by using an LNK format parser to extract the original file path from LNK files found in either `%USERPROFILE%\Recent` (Windows XP) or `%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Recent` (newer Windows versions) . Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat can enumerate files for encryption. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader recursively searches through directories for files. Software 'UPPERCUT': UPPERCUT has the capability to gather the victim's current directory. Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS identifies files with certain extensions from USB devices, then copies them to a predefined directory. Software 'DustySky': DustySky scans the victim for files that contain certain keywords and document types including PDF, DOC, DOCX, XLS, and XLSX, from a list that is obtained from the C2 as a text file. It can also identify logical drives for the infected machine. Software 'MESSAGETAP': MESSAGETAP checks for the existence of two configuration files (keyword_parm.txt and parm.txt) and attempts to read the files every 30 seconds. Software 'Babuk': Babuk has the ability to enumerate files on a targeted system. Software 'China Chopper': China Chopper's server component can list directory contents. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can identify files with .doc, .docx, .ppt, .pptx, .xls, .xlsx, and .pdf extensions and specific timestamps for collection. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can search for specific files on a compromised system. Software 'TYPEFRAME': TYPEFRAME can search directories for files on the victim’s machine. Software 'StealBit': StealBit can be configured to exfiltrate specific file types. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has enumerated directories containing vulnerability testing and cyber related content and facilities data such as construction drawings.Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has conducted key word searches within files and directories on a compromised hosts to identify files for exfiltration. Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 has used `vmtoolsd.exe` to enumerate files on guest machines. Group 'Patchwork': A Patchwork payload has searched all fixed drives on the victim for files matching a specified list of extensions. Group 'Sowbug': Sowbug identified and extracted all Word documents on a server by using a command containing * .doc and *.docx. The actors also searched for documents based on a specific date range and attempted to identify all installed software on a victim. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group macros can scan for Microsoft Word and Excel files to inject with additional malicious macros. Gamaredon Group has also used its backdoors to automatically list interesting files (such as Office documents) found on a system. Gamaredon Group has also identified directory trees, folders and files on the compromised host. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has searched for files within the victim environment for encryption and exfiltration. Medusa Group has also identified files associated with remote management services. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has used a batch script to gather folder and file names from victim hosts. Group 'APT38': APT38 have enumerated files and directories, or searched in specific locations within a compromised host. Group 'APT28': APT28 has used Forfiles to locate PDF, Excel, and Word documents during collection. The group also searched a compromised DCCC computer for specific terms. Group 'Inception': Inception used a file listing plugin to collect information about file and directories both on local and remote drives. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has run commands to check the content of folders on compromised hosts and has specifically targeted files with .doc, .ppt, .xls, .jtd, .eml, .xps, and .pdf extensions. During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has searched for and collected files on local and network drives. Group 'Velvet Ant': Velvet Ant has enumerated local files and folders on victim devices. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has utilized multiple commands to identify data of interest in file and directory listings. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following commands after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about files and directories: dir c:\ >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Documents and Settings" >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Program Files\" >> %temp%\download dir d:\ >> %temp%\download Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang uses command-line interaction to search files and directories. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has enumerated files on a compromised host. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used malware to collect information on files and directories. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider Spider enumerates a target organization for files and directories of interest, including source code, user provisioning, MFA device registration, network diagrams, and shared credentials in documents or spreadsheets. Group 'Aoqin Dragon': Aoqin Dragon has run scripts to identify file formats including Microsoft Word. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has monitored files' modified time. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal collected file listings of all default Windows directories. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware that checked if the ProgramData folder had folders or files with the keywords "Kasper," "Panda," or "ESET." Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware can list a victim's logical drives and the type, as well the total/free space of the fixed devices. Other malware can list a directory's contents. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used a script that checks `/proc/*/environ` for environment variables related to AWS. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used commands such as `dir` to examine the local filesystem of victim machines. Group 'LuminousMoth': LuminousMoth has used malware that scans for files in the Documents, Desktop, and Download folders and in other drives. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that looks for files and directories on the local file system. Group 'APT32': APT32's backdoor possesses the capability to list files and directories on a machine. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has searched the entire target system for DOC, DOCX, PPT, PPTX, XLS, XLSX, and PDF files. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used tools with the ability to search for files on a compromised host. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has run scripts to enumerate recently modified documents having either a .pdf, .doc, .docx, .xls or .xlsx extension. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware can use a common function to identify target files by their extension, and some also enumerate files and directories, including a Destover-like variant that lists files and gathers information for all drives.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has used malware that searched for files with specific patterns. Group 'Play': Play has used the Grixba information stealer to list security files and processes. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has searched compromised systems for folders of interest including those related to HR, audit and expense, and meeting memos. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has the ability to enumerate all files and directories on an infected system. Kimsuky has used a custom script with a function called CreateFileList() that can scan all filesystem drives, prioritizing C:\Users, to locate files and file extensions of interest that ultimately generates a file called `FileList.txt` saved within the victims %TEMP% Directory that contains the findings and the respective pathways. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has searched file contents on a compromised host. Group 'Confucius': Confucius has used a file stealer that checks the Document, Downloads, Desktop, and Picture folders for documents and images with specific extensions. Group 'APT41': APT41 has executed file /bin/pwd on exploited victims, perhaps to return architecture related information. Group 'Winnti Group': Winnti Group has used a program named ff.exe to search for specific documents on compromised hosts. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered malicious JavaScript payloads capable of listing folders and emails in exploited email servers. Group 'Windigo': Windigo has used a script to check for the presence of files created by OpenSSH backdoors. Group 'APT18': APT18 can list files information for specific directories. Group 'APT5': APT5 has used the BLOODMINE utility to discover files with .css, .jpg, .png, .gif, .ico, .js, and .jsp extensions in Pulse Secure Connect logs. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used WizTree to obtain network files and directory listings. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used a tool called MailSniper to search for files on the desktop and another utility called Sobolsoft to extract attachments from EML files. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used the Windows `dir` command to enumerate files and directories in a victim's network. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover files in specific locations on the hard disk %TEMP% directory, the current user's desktop, the Program Files directory, and Recent. Turla RPC backdoors have also searched for files matching the lPH*.dll pattern. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has collected a list of files from the victim and uploaded it to its C2 server, and then created a new list of specific files to steal. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors conducted a file listing discovery against multiple hosts to ensure locker encryption was successful. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors used a malicious DLL to search for files with specific keywords. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used `dir c:\\` to search for files. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors leveraged commands to locate accessible file shares, backup paths, or SharePoint content. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary leveraged Claude Code to identify sensitive data within the victim environment for extraction. Campaign 'Salesforce Data Exfiltration': During Salesforce Data Exfiltration, threat actors queried customers' Salesforce environments to identify sensitive information for exfiltration. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors gathered a recursive directory listing to find files and directories of interest. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Campaign 'Night Dragon': During Night Dragon, threat actors used zwShell to establish full remote control of the connected machine and browse the victim file system. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries obtained the contents of users’ directories using `dir /s /b C:\Users` command. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/.

T1518.001.name: Security Software Discovery
T1518.001.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of security software, configurations, defensive tools, and sensors that are installed on a system or in a cloud environment. This may include things such as cloud monitoring agents and anti-virus. Adversaries may use the information from [Security Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518/001) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Example commands that can be used to obtain security software information are [netsh](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0108), reg query with [Reg](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0075), dir with [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106), and [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057), but other indicators of discovery behavior may be more specific to the type of software or security system the adversary is looking for. It is becoming more common to see macOS malware perform checks for LittleSnitch and KnockKnock software. Adversaries may also utilize the [Cloud API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/009) to discover cloud-native security software installed on compute infrastructure, such as the AWS CloudWatch agent, Azure VM Agent, and Google Cloud Monitor agent. These agents may collect metrics and logs from the VM, which may be centrally aggregated in a cloud-based monitoring platform.
T1518.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Security Software Discovery Across Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0048': Adversary executes commands to enumerate installed antivirus, EDR, or firewall agents using WMI, registry queries, and built-in tools (e.g., tasklist, netsh, sc query). Correlated with elevated process privileges or scripting engine usage.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0049': Adversary runs discovery commands such as `ps aux`, `systemctl status`, or `cat /etc/init.d/` to enumerate security software or services. Often occurs alongside privilege escalation or bash script execution.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0050': Adversary attempts to detect monitoring agents such as Little Snitch, KnockKnock, or other system daemons via process listing (`ps -e`), application folder checks, and system extension listing.
T1518.001.procedures: Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to enumerate security software currently running on a system by process name of known products. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 can detect EDR userland hooks. Software 'Empire': Empire can enumerate antivirus software on the target. Software 'Pacu': Pacu can enumerate AWS security services, including WAF rules and GuardDuty detectors. Software 'netsh': netsh can be used to discover system firewall settings. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can determine if an anti-virus product is installed through the resolution of the service's virtual SID. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin attempts to identify security software running on the victim machine, such as BitDefender, Avast, and Kaspersky. Software 'Clop': Clop can search for processes with antivirus and antimalware product names. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to identify the installed anti-virus product on the compromised host. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has identified AV products on an infected host using the following command: `WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get displayName /Format:List`. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo collects a list of installed antivirus software from the victim’s system. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has identified drivers of AV solutions by searching for related filenames, keywords and signed certificates. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has checked for the presence of ESET antivirus applications `ekrn.exe` and `egui.exe`. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify the installed antivirus product on a targeted system. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia searches for anti-virus software and firewall products installed on the victim’s machine using WMI. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains a command to collect information about anti-virus software on the victim. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda checks to see if anti-virus, anti-spyware, or firewall products are installed in the victim’s environment. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has the ability to check for a specified list of security tools in the compromised environment. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has the capability to detect security solutions for termination or deletion within the victim device using hard-coded lists of strings containing security product executables. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has checked for a variety of antivirus products. Software 'Comnie': Comnie attempts to detect several anti-virus products. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can identify the installed antivirus engine. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has identified installed antivirus software on the system. Software 'CozyCar': The main CozyCar dropper checks whether the victim has an anti-virus product installed. If the installed product is on a predetermined list, the dropper will exit. Software 'Lumma Stealer': Lumma Stealer has detected antivirus processes using commands such as “tasklist” and “findstr.” Software 'SPAWNCHIMERA': SPAWNCHIMERA has checked where SELinux is enabled on the targeted host. Software 'ZxxZ': ZxxZ can search a compromised host to determine if it is running Windows Defender or Kasperky antivirus. Software 'PUNCHBUGGY': PUNCHBUGGY can gather AVs registered in the system. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can check for the Kaspersky Anti-Virus suite. Software 'POWERSTATS': POWERSTATS has detected security tools. Software 'FELIXROOT': FELIXROOT checks for installed security software like antivirus and firewall. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to identify which anti-virus products, firewalls, and anti-spyware products are in use. Software 'Mosquito': Mosquito's installer searches the Registry and system to see if specific antivirus tools are installed on the system. Software 'VERMIN': VERMIN uses WMI to check for anti-virus software installed on the system. Software 'Valak': Valak can determine if a compromised host has security products installed. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has the ability to check if /usr/sbin/setenforce exists. This file controls what mode SELinux is in. Software 'Flame': Flame identifies security software such as antivirus through the Security module. Software 'Astaroth': Astaroth checks for the presence of Avast antivirus in the C:\Program\Files\ folder. Software 'Bumblebee': Bumblebee can identify specific analytical tools based on running processes. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to identify installed anti-virus products on a compromised host. Software 'POWRUNER': POWRUNER may collect information on the victim's anti-virus software. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can collect a list of anti-virus products installed on a machine. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can detect Avast Software, Doctor Web, Kaspersky, AVG, ESET, and Sophos antivirus programs. Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya determines if specific antivirus programs are running on an infected host machine. Software 'YAHOYAH': YAHOYAH checks for antimalware solution processes on the system. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can identify security software. Software 'Netwalker': Netwalker can detect and terminate active security software-related processes on infected systems. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can check for running processes on the victim’s machine to look for Kaspersky and Bitdefender antivirus products. Software 'PureCrypter': PureCrypter can identify installed antivirus solutions. Software 'Ferocious': Ferocious has checked for AV software as part of its persistence process. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can list installed security products including the Trusteer and Diebold Warsaw GAS Tecnologia online banking protections. Software 'T9000': T9000 performs checks for various antivirus and security products during installation. Software 'CHOPSTICK': CHOPSTICK checks for antivirus and forensics software. Software 'HiddenFace': HiddenFace can identify processes identified with security applications and tooling. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to detect anti-virus products and processes on a compromised host. Software 'build_downer': build_downer has the ability to detect if the infected host is running an anti-virus process. Software 'FlawedAmmyy': FlawedAmmyy will attempt to detect anti-virus products during the initial infection. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can recognize the presence of monitoring tools on a target system. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can identify if ESET or BitDefender antivirus are installed before dropping its payload. Software 'StoneDrill': StoneDrill can check for antivirus and antimalware programs. Software 'IcedID': IcedID can identify AV products on an infected host using the following command: ` WMIC.exe WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get * /Format:List`. Software 'Carberp': Carberp has queried the infected system's registry searching for specific registry keys associated with antivirus products. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte checks for the presence of various security software products during execution. Software 'xCaon': xCaon has checked for the existence of Kaspersky antivirus software on the system. Software 'Felismus': Felismus checks for processes associated with anti-virus vendors. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can check for the presence of specific security products. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can list security software, such as by using WMIC to identify anti-virus products installed on the victim’s machine and to obtain firewall details. Software 'Kasidet': Kasidet has the ability to identify any anti-virus installed on the infected system. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information from the victim about installed anti-virus software. Software 'RogueRobin': RogueRobin enumerates running processes to search for Wireshark and Windows Sysinternals suite. Software 'Waterbear': Waterbear can find the presence of a specific security software. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet enumerates the currently running processes related to a variety of security products. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu can list installed security products in the victim’s environment. Software 'JPIN': JPIN checks for the presence of certain security-related processes and deletes its installer/uninstaller component if it identifies any of them. Software 'RustyWater': RustyWater has attempted to detect more than 25 antivirus and EDR tools. Software 'DustySky': DustySky checks for the existence of anti-virus. Software 'ROAMINGHOUSE': ROAMINGHOUSE can identify McAfee applications on compromised hosts and change its execution method if one is detected. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus has the ability to identify installed antivirus products. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP is capable of checking whether a compromised device is running DeepFreeze by Faronics. Software 'Wingbird': Wingbird checks for the presence of Bitdefender security software. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate looks for various security products by process name using hard-coded values in the malware. DarkGate will not execute its keylogging thread if a process name associated with Trend Micro anti-virus is identified, or if runtime checks identify the presence of Kaspersky anti-virus. DarkGate will initiate a new thread if certain security products are identified on the victim, and recreate any malicious files associated with it if it determines they were removed by security software in a new system location. Software 'Lizar': Lizar can search for processes associated with an anti-virus product from list. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET searches firewall configuration files located in /Library/Preferences/ and uses csrutil status to determine if System Integrity Protection is enabled. Software 'More_eggs': More_eggs can obtain information on installed anti-malware programs. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has checked for the presence of "Little Snitch", macOS network monitoring and application firewall software, stopping and exiting if it is found. Software 'LiteDuke': LiteDuke has the ability to check for the presence of Kaspersky security software. Software 'BadPatch': BadPatch uses WMI to enumerate installed security products in the victim’s environment. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can search for a variety of security software programs, EDR systems, and malware analysis tools. Software 'EVILNUM': EVILNUM can search for anti-virus products on the system. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST checked for a variety of antivirus/endpoint detection agents prior to execution. Software 'TAMECAT': TAMECAT has used Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to check for anti-virus products. Software 'ThiefQuest': ThiefQuest uses the kill_unwanted function to get a list of running processes, compares each process with an encrypted list of “unwanted” security related programs, and kills the processes for security related programs. Software 'Epic': Epic searches for anti-malware services running on the victim’s machine and terminates itself if it finds them. Software 'RTM': RTM can obtain information about security software on the victim. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman can search for anti-virus products on the system. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher probes the system to check for antimalware processes. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can check for the presence of network sniffers, AV, and BitDefender firewall. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon checks for anti-malware products and processes. Software 'StreamEx': StreamEx has the ability to scan for security tools such as firewalls and antivirus tools. Software 'AuTo Stealer': AuTo Stealer has the ability to collect information about installed AV products from an infected host. Software 'Meteor': Meteor has the ability to search for Kaspersky Antivirus on a victim's machine. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT can identify AV products on an infected host using the following command: `cmd.exe WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get displayName /Format:List`. Software 'EvilBunny': EvilBunny has been observed querying installed antivirus software. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can check for the presence of 29 different antivirus tools. Software 'Remsec': Remsec has a plugin detect security products via active drivers. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can identify the processes for Bkav antivirus. Software 'MoleNet': MoleNet can use WMI commands to check the system for firewall and antivirus software. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware looks for security software products prior to full execution. Software 'PipeMon': PipeMon can check for the presence of ESET and Kaspersky security software. Software 'LitePower': LitePower can identify installed AV software. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb has run shell commands to obtain a list of installed security products. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has searched for anti-malware strings and anti-virus processes running on the system. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used the Windows service winmgmts:\\.\root\SecurityCenter2 to check installed antivirus products. Group 'SideCopy': SideCopy uses a loader DLL file to collect AV product names from an infected host. Group 'Windshift': Windshift has used malware to identify installed AV and commonly used forensic and malware analysis tools. Group 'APT38': APT38 has identified security software, configurations, defensive tools, and sensors installed on a compromised system. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used Registry keys to detect and avoid executing in potential sandboxes. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to check for anti-virus products. Group 'TA2541': TA2541 has used tools to search victim systems for security products such as antivirus and firewall software. Group 'The White Company': The White Company has checked for specific antivirus products on the target’s computer, including Kaspersky, Quick Heal, AVG, BitDefender, Avira, Sophos, Avast!, and ESET. Group 'Naikon': Naikon uses commands such as netsh advfirewall firewall to discover local firewall settings. Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group used a JavaScript backdoor that is capable of collecting a list of the security solutions installed on the victim's machine. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group has used PowerShell scripts to identify security software on the victim machine. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used WMI to identify anti-virus products installed on a victim's machine. Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork scanned the “Program Files” directories for a directory with the string “Total Security” (the installation path of the “360 Total Security” antivirus tool). Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware to check running processes against a hard-coded list of security tools often used by malware researchers. Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has detected endpoint security solutions using `sc query sense` and `sc query windefend`. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to discover third party endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools on compromised systems. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has checked for the presence of antivirus software with powershell Get-CimInstance -Namespace root/securityCenter2 – classname antivirusproduct. Kimsuky has also obtained details on antivirus software through WMI queries using `Win32_OperatingSystem` and `SecurityCenter2.AntiVirusProduct`. Kimsuky has also checked the status of Windows Defender through the use `cmd /s sc query WinDefend`. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for security products on infected machines. Group 'Malteiro': Malteiro collects the installed antivirus on the victim machine. Group 'Turla': Turla has obtained information on security software, including security logging information that may indicate whether their malware has been detected. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat can determine is Kaspersky software is running on an endpoint by running `cmd /c wmic process where name="avp.exe"`. Group 'Play': Play has used the information-stealing tool Grixba to scan for anti-virus software. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper can search for anti-virus software running on the system. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has detected security solutions for termination or deletion within the victim device using hard-coded lists of strings containing security product executables. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte enumerated installed security products during operations. Group 'Rocke': Rocke used scripts which detected and uninstalled antivirus software. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity involved removal of security tools, as well as other identified IOT malware, from compromised devices. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used scripts to detect security software. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used WMI queries to determine if analysis tools were running on a compromised system. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity involved removal of security tools, as well as other identified IOT malware, from compromised devices.

T1007.name: System Service Discovery
T1007.description: Adversaries may try to gather information about registered local system services. Adversaries may obtain information about services using tools as well as OS utility commands such as sc query, tasklist /svc, systemctl --type=service, and net start. Adversaries may also gather information about schedule tasks via commands such as `schtasks` on Windows or `crontab -l` on Linux and macOS.(Citation: Elastic Security Labs GOSAR 2024)(Citation: SentinelLabs macOS Malware 2021)(Citation: Splunk Linux Gormir 2024)(Citation: Aquasec Kinsing 2020) Adversaries may use the information from [System Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1007) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions.
T1007.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of System Service Discovery Commands Across OS Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 1325': Enumeration of services via native CLI tools (e.g., `sc query`, `tasklist /svc`, `net start`) or API calls via PowerShell and WMI.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1326': Execution of service management commands like `systemctl list-units`, `service --status-all`, or direct reading of `/etc/init.d`.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1327': Discovery via launchctl commands, or process enumeration using `ps aux | grep com.apple.` to identify daemons and services.
T1007.procedures: Software 'Net': The net start command can be used in Net to find information about Windows services. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate service and service permission information. Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to discover services running on a system. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can search for modifiable services that could be used for privilege escalation. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has gathered information about running services. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use net start to display running services. Software 'Comnie': Comnie runs the command: net start >> %TEMP%\info.dat on a victim. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can collect a list of services on a victim machine. Software 'Elise': Elise executes net start after initial communication is made to the remote server. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA has the capability to enumerate services. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has leveraged an encoded list of services that it designates for termination. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of running services with the command tasklist /svc. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can list local services. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA uses tasklist /svc to display running tasks. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT can query service configuration information. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can create and register a service for execution. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can enumerate services on compromised hosts. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer queries the system to identify existing services. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can enumerate services on the victim machine. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can check if it is running as a service on a compromised host. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can identify specific services for termination or to be left running at execution. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can obtain a list of the services from a system. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can obtain running services on the victim. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy enumerates all Windows services. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST collected a list of service names that were hashed using a FNV-1a + XOR algorithm to check against similarly-hashed hardcoded blocklists. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can list all services and their configurations. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can check whether the service name `FAX` is present. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has obtained active services running on the victim’s system through the functions `OpenSCManagerW()` and `EnumServicesStatusExW()`. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can monitor services. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT has a feature to list the available services on the system. Software 'REvil': REvil can enumerate active services. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can query service status using QueryServiceStatusEx function. Software 'SynAck': SynAck enumerates all running services. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to execute the `net start` command. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to retrieve a list of services on the infected host. Software 'S-Type': S-Type runs the command net start on a victim. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information on programs and services on the victim that are configured to automatically run at startup. Software 'ZLib': ZLib has the ability to discover and manipulate Windows services. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can retrieve information about a compromised system's running services. Software 'Emissary': Emissary has the capability to execute the command net start to interact with services. Software 'Babuk': Babuk can enumerate all services running on a compromised host. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list running services. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can enumerate services on a victim machine. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate services. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the tasklist /svc command to list the services on the system. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can enumerate existing Windows services on the host that are configured to run as LocalSystem. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot collects a list of install programs and services on the system’s machine. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has leveraged `tasklist` to gather running services on victim host. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can gather service information on targeted systems. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can list running services. Software 'Dyre': Dyre has the ability to identify running services on a compromised host. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell can check the services on the system. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used TROJ_GETVERSION to discover system services. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for services such as Alibaba Cloud Security's aliyun service and BMC Helix Cloud Security's bmc-agent service in order to disable them. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used the win32_service WMI class to retrieve a list of services from the system. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used sc query on a victim to gather information about services. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover running services and associated processes using the tasklist /svc command. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to discover services for third party EDR products. Group 'Poseidon Group': After compromising a victim, Poseidon Group discovers all running services. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used an instrumentor script to gather the names of all services running on a victim's system. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used net start and net use for system service discovery. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang performs service discovery using net start commands. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Tasklist for discovery post compromise. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Tasklist to obtain information from a compromised host. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used `net start` to list running services. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following command following exploitation of a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about services: net start >> %temp%\download Group 'APT1': APT1 used the commands net start and tasklist to get a listing of the services on the system. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used the `tasklist` command to search for one of its backdoors. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `net start` command as part of their initial reconnaissance.

T1518.002.name: Backup Software Discovery
T1518.002.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of backup software or configurations that are installed on a system. Adversaries may use this information to shape follow-on behaviors, such as [Data Destruction](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1485), [Inhibit System Recovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1490), or [Data Encrypted for Impact](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1486).  

Commands that can be used to obtain security software information are [netsh](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0108), `reg query` with [Reg](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0075), `dir` with [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106), and [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057), but other indicators of discovery behavior may be more specific to the type of software or security system the adversary is looking for, such as Veeam, Acronis, Dropbox, or Paragon.(Citation: Symantec Play Ransomware 2023)
T1518.002.detections:
Detection Strategy: Backup Software Discovery via CLI, Registry, and Process Inspection (T1518.002)
- Analytic 'Analytic 0240': Defender observes execution of commands like `tasklist`, `sc query`, `reg query`, or PowerShell WMI/Registry queries targeting known backup products (e.g., Veeam, Acronis, CrashPlan). Behavior often includes parent-child lineage involving PowerShell or cmd.exe with discovery syntax, and enumeration of services, directories, or registry paths tied to backup software.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0241': Defender observes use of CLI tools (`find`, `grep`, `ls`, `dpkg`, `rpm`, `systemctl`, `ps aux`) to discover backup agents or config files (e.g., rsnapshot, duplicity, veeam). This often includes command lines that recursively search `/etc/`, `/opt/`, or `/var/` directories for keywords like `backup`, and parent-child relationships involving shell or Python scripts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0242': Defender detects execution of `mdfind`, `launchctl`, or GUI-based enumeration (e.g., `/Applications/Time Machine.app`) along with command-line usage of `find`, `grep`, or `system_profiler` to identify installed backup tools like Time Machine, Carbon Copy Cloner, or Backblaze. Often triggered from Terminal sessions or within post-exploitation scripts.
T1518.002.procedures: Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has utilized the PowerShell script `Get-DataInfo.ps1` to collect installed backup software information from a compromised machine.

T1552.002.name: Credentials in Registry
T1552.002.description: Adversaries may search the Registry on compromised systems for insecurely stored credentials. The Windows Registry stores configuration information that can be used by the system or other programs. Adversaries may query the Registry looking for credentials and passwords that have been stored for use by other programs or services. Sometimes these credentials are used for automatic logons. Example commands to find Registry keys related to password information: (Citation: Pentestlab Stored Credentials) * Local Machine Hive: reg query HKLM /f password /t REG_SZ /s * Current User Hive: reg query HKCU /f password /t REG_SZ /s
T1552.002.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Password Policies': Do not store credentials within the Registry.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': If it is necessary that software must store credentials in the Registry, then ensure the associated accounts have limited permissions so they cannot be abused if obtained by an adversary.
Mitigation 'Audit': Proactively search for credentials within the Registry and attempt to remediate the risk.
T1552.002.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detect Credential Discovery via Windows Registry Enumeration
- Analytic 'Analytic 0694': Defenders observe command-line executions or API-based registry reads targeting sensitive paths like HKLM or HKCU with keyword filters such as 'password', 'cred', or 'logon'. Typically performed by Reg.exe, PowerShell, custom binaries, or offensive tools such as Cobalt Strike. Correlation with process ancestry and command-line arguments indicates suspicious credential discovery activity.
T1552.002.procedures: Software 'Reg': Reg may be used to find credentials in the Windows Registry. Software 'PowerSploit': PowerSploit has several modules that search the Windows Registry for stored credentials: Get-UnattendedInstallFile, Get-Webconfig, Get-ApplicationHost, Get-SiteListPassword, Get-CachedGPPPassword, and Get-RegistryAutoLogon. Software 'IceApple': IceApple can harvest credentials from local and remote host registries. Software 'StrelaStealer': StrelaStealer enumerates the registry key `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\16.0\Outlook\Profiles\Outlook\9375CFF0413111d3B88A00104B2A6676\` to identify the values for "IMAP User," "IMAP Server," and "IMAP Password" associated with the Outlook email application. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot has retrieved PuTTY credentials by querying the Software\SimonTatham\Putty\Sessions registry key Software 'Valak': Valak can use the clientgrabber module to steal e-mail credentials from the Registry. Software 'Agent Tesla': Agent Tesla has the ability to extract credentials from the Registry. Group 'APT32': APT32 used Outlook Credential Dumper to harvest credentials stored in Windows registry. Group 'VOID MANTICORE': VOID MANTICORE had exported credentials from registry hives to include those stored in HKLM. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl used LaZagne to obtain passwords in the Registry.

T1010.name: Application Window Discovery
T1010.description:
Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of open application windows. Window listings could convey information about how the system is used.(Citation: Prevailion DarkWatchman 2021) For example, information about application windows could be used identify potential data to collect as well as identifying security tooling ([Security Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518/001)) to evade.(Citation: ESET Grandoreiro April 2020)

Adversaries typically abuse system features for this type of enumeration. For example, they may gather information through native system features such as [Command and Scripting Interpreter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059) commands and [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106) functions.
T1010.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Application Window Enumeration via API or Scripting
- Analytic 'Analytic 0271': Processes using Win32 API calls (e.g., EnumWindows, GetForegroundWindow) or scripting tools (e.g., PowerShell, VBScript) to enumerate open windows. These often appear with reconnaissance or data collection TTPs.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0272': Scripted or binary usage of X11 utilities (e.g., xdotool, wmctrl) or direct /proc/*/window mappings to discover open GUI windows and active desktops.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0273': Processes that utilize AppleScript, `CGWindowListCopyWindowInfo`, or `NSRunningApplication` APIs to list active application windows and foreground processes.
T1010.procedures: Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can enumerate the active Window during keylogging through execution of `GetActiveWindowTitle`. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can list all windows on victim systems. Software 'QuasarRAT': APT-C-36 used a customized version of QuasarRAT to monitor browser windows for strings relating to specific Colombian financial institutions. Software 'Attor': Attor can obtain application window titles and then determines which windows to perform Screen Capture on. Software 'NetTraveler': NetTraveler reports window names along with keylogger information to provide application context. Software 'Cadelspy': Cadelspy has the ability to identify open windows on the compromised host. Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro can check the name of the window displayed on the system. Software 'njRAT': njRAT gathers information about opened windows during the initial infection. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can monitor the titles of open windows to identify specific keywords. Software 'DarkGate': DarkGate will search for cryptocurrency wallets by examining application window names for specific strings. DarkGate extracts information collected via NirSoft tools from the hosting process's memory by first identifying the window through the FindWindow API function. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman reports window names along with keylogger information to provide application context. Software 'PowerDuke': PowerDuke has a command to get text of the current foreground window. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can enumerate windows and child windows on a compromised host. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to identify the titles of running windows on a compromised host. Software 'Grandoreiro': Grandoreiro can identify installed security tools based on window names. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has used `GetForegroundWindow` to detect virtualization or sandboxes by calling the API twice and comparing each window handle. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate active windows. Software 'Machete': Machete saves the window names. Software 'NightClub': NightClub can use `GetForegroundWindow` to enumerate the active window. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE can discover and close windows on controlled systems. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar gathers information about opened windows. Software 'Remexi': Remexi has a command to capture active windows on the machine and retrieve window titles. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate running application windows. Software 'SOUNDBITE': SOUNDBITE is capable of enumerating application windows. Software 'Duqu': The discovery modules used with Duqu can collect information on open windows. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to list the names of all open windows on the infected host. Software 'PAKLOG': PAKLOG has used `GetForegroundWindow` to access the foreground window. PAKLOG has also captured text from the foreground windows. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo can enumerate all windows on the victim’s machine. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT can use the `GetForegroundWindow` and `GetWindowText` APIs to discover where the user is typing. Software 'Catchamas': Catchamas obtains application windows titles and then determines which windows to perform Screen Capture on. Software 'QakBot': QakBot has the ability to enumerate windows on a compromised host. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has the ability to list open windows on the compromised host. Software 'PoisonIvy': PoisonIvy captures window titles. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream has the ability to discover application windows via execution of `EnumWindows`. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware IndiaIndia obtains and sends to its C2 server the title of the window for each running process. The KilaAlfa keylogger also reports the title of the window in the foreground. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has collected window title information from compromised systems. Group 'HEXANE': HEXANE has used a PowerShell-based keylogging tool to capture the window title.

T1613.name: Container and Resource Discovery
T1613.description:
Adversaries may attempt to discover containers and other resources that are available within a containers environment. Other resources may include images, deployments, pods, nodes, and other information such as the status of a cluster.

These resources can be viewed within web applications such as the Kubernetes dashboard or can be queried via the Docker and Kubernetes APIs.(Citation: Docker API)(Citation: Kubernetes API) In Docker, logs may leak information about the environment, such as the environment’s configuration, which services are available, and what cloud provider the victim may be utilizing. The discovery of these resources may inform an adversary’s next steps in the environment, such as how to perform lateral movement and which methods to utilize for execution.
T1613.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Deny direct remote access to internal systems through the use of network proxies, gateways, and firewalls.
Mitigation 'Limit Access to Resource Over Network': Limit communications with the container service to managed and secured channels, such as local Unix sockets or remote access via SSH. Require secure port access to communicate with the APIs over TLS by disabling unauthenticated access to the Docker API and Kubernetes API Server. In Kubernetes clusters deployed in cloud environments, use native cloud platform features to restrict the IP ranges that are permitted to access to API server. Where possible, consider enabling just-in-time (JIT) access to the Kubernetes API to place additional restrictions on access.
Mitigation 'User Account Management': Enforce the principle of least privilege by limiting dashboard visibility to only the required users. When using Kubernetes, avoid giving users wildcard permissions or adding users to the `system:masters` group, and use `RoleBindings` rather than `ClusterRoleBindings` to limit user privileges to specific namespaces.
T1613.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Container and Resource Discovery
- Analytic 'Analytic 1352': Detection of adversary attempts to enumerate containers, pods, nodes, and related resources within containerized environments. Defenders may observe anomalous API calls to Docker or Kubernetes (e.g., 'docker ps', 'kubectl get pods', 'kubectl get nodes'), unusual account activity against the Kubernetes dashboard, or unexpected queries against container metadata endpoints. These events should be correlated with user context and network activity to reveal resource discovery attempts.
T1613.procedures: Software 'Peirates': Peirates can enumerate Kubernetes pods in a given namespace. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has used masscan to search for kubelets and the kubelet API for additional running containers. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has checked for running containers with docker ps and for specific container names with docker inspect. TeamTNT has also searched for Kubernetes pods running in a local network.

T1018.name: Remote System Discovery
T1018.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of other systems by IP address, hostname, or other logical identifier on a network that may be used for Lateral Movement from the current system. Functionality could exist within remote access tools to enable this, but utilities available on the operating system could also be used such as [Ping](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0097), net view using [Net](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0039), or, on ESXi servers, `esxcli network diag ping`. Adversaries may also analyze data from local host files (ex: C:\Windows\System32\Drivers\etc\hosts or /etc/hosts) or other passive means (such as local [Arp](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0099) cache entries) in order to discover the presence of remote systems in an environment. Adversaries may also target discovery of network infrastructure as well as leverage [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) commands on network devices to gather detailed information about systems within a network (e.g. show cdp neighbors, show arp).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A)(Citation: CISA AR21-126A FIVEHANDS May 2021)
T1018.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection Strategy for Remote System Enumeration Behavior
- Analytic 'Analytic 1583': Execution of network enumeration utilities (e.g., net.exe, ping.exe, tracert.exe) in short succession, often chained with lateral movement tools or system enumeration commands.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1584': Use of bash scripts or interactive shells to issue sequential ping, arp, or traceroute commands to map remote hosts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1585': Execution of built-in or AppleScript-based system enumeration via `arp`, `netstat`, `ping`, and discovery of `/etc/hosts` contents.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1586': ESXi shell or SSH access issuing `esxcli network diag ping` or viewing routing tables to identify connected hosts.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1587': Execution of discovery commands like `show cdp neighbors`, `show arp`, and other interface-level introspection on Cisco or Juniper devices.
T1018.procedures: Software 'ROADTools': ROADTools can enumerate Azure AD systems and devices. Software 'Net': Commands such as net view can be used in Net to gather information about available remote systems. Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can discover active IP addresses, along with the machine name, within a targeted network. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can enumerate and collect the properties of domain computers. Software 'BloodHound': BloodHound can enumerate and collect the properties of domain computers, including domain controllers. Software 'Ping': Ping can be used to identify remote systems within a network. Software 'Arp': Arp can be used to display a host's ARP cache, which may include address resolutions for remote systems. Software 'NBTscan': NBTscan can list NetBIOS computer names. Software 'Nltest': Nltest may be used to enumerate remote domain controllers using options such as /dclist and /dsgetdc. Software 'AdFind': AdFind has the ability to query Active Directory for computers. Software 'MURKYTOP': MURKYTOP has the capability to identify remote hosts on connected networks. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': The TAINTEDSCRIBE command and execution module can perform target system enumeration. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can use net view to discover remote systems. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify remote systems through the net view command. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can use net view to gather information about remote systems. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate remote systems using Net View. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can use a PowerShell object such as, `System.Net.NetworkInformation.Ping` to ping a computer. Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat can broadcasts NetBIOS Name Service (NBNC) messages to search for servers connected to compromised networks. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can use LDAP queries to connect to AD and iterate over connected workstations. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO can run `net view` and `net view /domain` for network discovery. Software 'yty': yty uses the net view command for discovery. Software 'njRAT': njRAT can identify remote hosts on connected networks. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer can enumerate remote computers in the compromised network. Software 'Remsec': Remsec can ping or traceroute a remote host. Software 'Gomir': Gomir probes arbitrary network endpoints for TCP connectivity. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT used Nmap for remote system discovery. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub can enumerate all accessible machines from the infected system. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the net view command on the victim’s machine. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of available servers with the command net view. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can collect information about hosts on the victim network. Software 'Comnie': Comnie runs the net view command Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry scans its local network segment for remote systems to try to exploit and copy itself to. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use net view /domain to display hostnames of available systems on a network. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike uses the native Windows Network Enumeration APIs to interrogate and discover targets in a Windows Active Directory network. Software 'DRATzarus': DRATzarus can search for other machines connected to compromised host and attempt to map the network. Software 'OSInfo': OSInfo performs a connection test to discover remote systems in the network Software 'Flagpro': Flagpro has been used to execute net view on a targeted system. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA runs the net view /domain and net view commands. Software 'SHOTPUT': SHOTPUT has a command to list all servers in the domain, as well as one to locate domain controllers on a domain. Software 'MgBot': MgBot includes modules for performing ARP scans of local connected systems. Software 'Carbon': Carbon uses the net view command. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used a script to parse files like /etc/hosts and SSH known_hosts to discover remote systems. Software 'Diavol': Diavol can use the ARP table to find remote hosts to scan. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot can enumerate computers and network devices. Software 'SpicyOmelette': SpicyOmelette can identify payment systems, payment gateways, and ATM systems in compromised environments. Software 'Olympic Destroyer': Olympic Destroyer uses Windows Management Instrumentation to enumerate all systems in the network. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can enumerate domain-connected hosts during its discovery phase. Software 'Shamoon': Shamoon scans the C-class subnet of the IPs on the victim's interfaces. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can use `ping` to identify remote hosts within the victim network. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea can enumerate and map ICS-specific systems in victim environments. Software 'Havoc': Havoc features a module capable of host enumeration. Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used tools such as Nmap and MASSCAN for remote service discovery. Group 'menuPass': menuPass uses scripts to enumerate IP ranges on the victim network. menuPass has also issued the command net view /domain to a PlugX implant to gather information about remote systems on the network. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used Angry IP Scanner to detect remote systems. Group 'Agrius': Agrius used the tool NBTscan to scan for remote, accessible hosts in victim environments. Group 'Naikon': Naikon has used a netbios scanner for remote machine identification. Group 'Deep Panda': Deep Panda has used ping to identify other machines of interest. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca used the command powershell “Get-EventLog -LogName security -Newest 500 | where {$_.EventID -eq 4624} | format-list - property * | findstr “Address”” to find the network information of successfully logged-in accounts to discovery addresses of other machines. Earth Lusca has also used multiple scanning tools to discover other machines within the same compromised network. Group 'APT41': APT41 has used MiPing to discover active systems in the victim network. Group 'FIN5': FIN5 has used the open source tool Essential NetTools to map the network and build a list of targets. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used multiple methods, including Ping, to enumerate systems on compromised networks. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has used a tool to query Active Directory using LDAP, discovering information about computers listed in AD. During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team remotely discovered systems over LAN connections. OT systems were visible from the IT network as well, giving adversaries the ability to discover operational assets. During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team checked for connectivity to resources within the network and used LDAP to query Active Directory, discovering information about computers listed in AD. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has queried Active Directory for computers using AdFind. Mustang Panda has also utilized SharpNBTScan to scan the victim environment. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Ping for system discovery. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has used the net view command. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used Ping to identify remote systems. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER typically use ping and Net to enumerate systems. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used NBTscan and custom tools to discover remote systems. Group 'APT32': APT32 has enumerated DC servers using the command net group "Domain Controllers" /domain. The group has also used the ping command. Group 'Silence': Silence has used Nmap to scan the corporate network, build a network topology, and identify vulnerable hosts. Group 'FIN6': FIN6 used publicly available tools (including Microsoft's built-in SQL querying tool, osql.exe) to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance against Active Directory, Structured Query Language (SQL) servers, and NetBIOS. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used PowerView to enumerate all Windows Server, Windows Server 2003, and Windows 7 instances in the Active Directory database. Turla has also used net group "Domain Computers" /domain, net group "Domain Controllers" /domain, and net group "Exchange Servers" /domain to enumerate domain computers, including the organization's DC and Exchange Server. Group 'Rocke': Rocke has looked for IP addresses in the known_hosts file on the infected system and attempted to SSH into them. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used Microsoft’s Sysinternals tools to gather detailed information about remote systems. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has utilized various scans and queries to find domain controllers and remote services in the target environment. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has enumerated domain controllers using `net group "Domain computers"` and `nltest /dclist`. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used networkdll for network discovery and psfin specifically for financial and point of sale indicators. Wizard Spider has also used AdFind, nltest/dclist, and PowerShell script Get-DataInfo.ps1 to enumerate domain computers, including the domain controller. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that can detect the existence of remote systems. Group 'GALLIUM': GALLIUM used a modified version of NBTscan to identify available NetBIOS name servers over the network as well as ping to identify remote systems. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte used tools such as Arp to identify remotely-connected devices. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used dsquery and other Active Directory utilities to enumerate hosts; they have also used nltest.exe /dclist to retrieve a list of domain controllers. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has likely obtained a list of hosts in the victim environment. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan performed extensive remote host enumeration to build their own map of victim networks during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used AdFind to enumerate remote systems. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `net view` and `ping` commands as part of their advanced reconnaissance. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used `nbtscan` and `ping` to discover remote systems, as well as `dsquery subnet` on a domain controller to retrieve all subnets in the Active Directory. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used several tools and batch files to map victims' internal networks. Campaign 'Leviathan Australian Intrusions': Leviathan performed extensive remote host enumeration to build their own map of victim networks during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Campaign '2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team remotely discovered systems over LAN connections. OT systems were visible from the IT network as well, giving adversaries the ability to discover operational assets. Campaign 'Operation Digital Eye': During Operation Digital Eye, threat actors used Ping for reconnaissance. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 used AdFind to enumerate remote systems. Campaign '2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack': During the 2016 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team checked for connectivity to resources within the network and used LDAP to query Active Directory, discovering information about computers listed in AD. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors used the commands `net view /all /domain` and `ping` to discover remote systems.
